mission.title: Install and Configure Ingress NGINX Controller
mission.description: Ingress NGINX Controller is an Ingress controller for Kubernetes that uses NGINX as a reverse proxy and load balancer. It is suitable for managing external access to services in a Kubernetes cluster, but note that it is being retired and should not be deployed in new environments. Key trade-offs include its lack of ongoing development and potential issues in multi-tenant environments.

steps.title: Add the Ingress NGINX Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Ingress NGINX Helm chart repository to your Helm client.

```bash

helm repo add ingress-nginx https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/ingress-nginx/main/deploy/helm-chart/ingress-nginx

```

> This URL points to the official Helm chart repository.

steps.title: Install the Ingress NGINX Controller
steps.description:
This command installs the Ingress NGINX Controller in the 'ingress-nginx' namespace using the specified version.

```bash

helm install ingress-nginx ingress-nginx/ingress-nginx --namespace ingress-nginx --create-namespace --version 4.10.1

```

> Ensure to use the correct version of the Helm chart.

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
This command checks if the Ingress NGINX pods are running successfully.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx

```

> Look for the status of the pods to ensure they are in 'Running' state.

steps.title: Create an example Ingress resource
steps.description:
This step creates a sample Ingress resource to test the Ingress NGINX Controller.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: example-ingress
  namespace: default
spec:
  rules:
  - host: example.local
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: example-service
            port:
              number: 80
EOF

```

> Replace 'example.local' and 'example-service' with your actual values.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: example-ingress
  namespace: default
spec:
  rules:
  - host: example.local
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: example-service
            port:
              number: 80
EOF

steps.title: Verify the Ingress resource
steps.description:
This command checks if the Ingress resource is created and ready.

```bash

kubectl get ingress example-ingress

```

> Ensure that the Ingress resource is listed and has the correct address.

uninstall.title: Uninstall the Ingress NGINX Controller
uninstall.description:
This command removes the Ingress NGINX Controller and all associated resources. Be aware that this will delete the Ingress resources and any associated load balancers.

```bash

helm uninstall ingress-nginx --namespace ingress-nginx

kubectl delete namespace ingress-nginx

```

> This will remove the Ingress NGINX installation.
> This will delete the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the Ingress NGINX Controller
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you backup your current configuration. This step will upgrade the Ingress NGINX Controller to a newer version.

```bash

kubectl get ingress -n ingress-nginx -o yaml > ingress-backup.yaml

helm upgrade ingress-nginx ingress-nginx/ingress-nginx --namespace ingress-nginx --version 4.10.2

kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx

```

> Backup your existing Ingress resources.
> Upgrade to the specified version.
> Verify that the pods are running after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl get ingress -n ingress-nginx -o yaml > ingress-backup.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'no matches for kind "Ingress" in version "networking.k8s.io/v1"'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the Ingress resource is not supported in your Kubernetes version.

**Fix:**
Ensure your Kubernetes version is 1.19 or later, or adjust the Ingress resource to match the supported API version.

troubleshooting.title: Ingress resource not routing traffic as expected.
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the Ingress rules are misconfigured or the backend service is not reachable.

**Fix:**
Check the Ingress rules and ensure the backend service is running and accessible.

troubleshooting.title: Pods in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state.
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This typically indicates an issue with the container configuration or resource limits.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the Ingress NGINX pods using 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n ingress-nginx' for more details.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'service "<service-name>" not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the specified service in the Ingress resource does not exist.

**Fix:**
Verify that the service name and port in the Ingress resource are correct and that the service is deployed.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for custom error pages and enhanced logging capabilities.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated the use of annotations for certain configurations in favor of ConfigMap.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: Review and update your Ingress configurations to use the new ConfigMap settings.

resolution.summary: You should see the Ingress resource listed with the correct address when running 'kubectl get ingress'. The output should show the Ingress name, host, and backend service details.
codeSnippets:

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: example-ingress
  namespace: default
spec:

  - host: example.local
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: example-service
            port:
              number: 80
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the specified resources and the necessary tools installed.
